title: Why New Smartphone Cameras Feel Worse

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: All right, so I decided to try something. I broke out every single iPhone generation from one to 17 and took the same picture with every single one of them, back to back. And the results were super interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I did this with a couple of different phones in a couple of different scenarios. And what ended up surprising me and quite a few other people is it doesn't always look like the latest generation is always far and away the best. And I'll tell you why.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: See for years, smartphone cameras have been focused on getting better and better for regular photos, right? Because they were bad, of course, when they first started. The first iPhone camera was almost an afterthought. It was this little pinhole two-megapixel webcam-type of thing, no autofocus, no video recording, didn't even have a selfie camera.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But as people and culture valued the ability to take quick high-quality pictures and videos more and more, of course you see the cameras improving drastically on the back of our phones year over year. It would be a legitimate selling point that a camera on a new generation of phone is way better than the year before.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so this continued as phones got bigger and better and cameras got a bit bigger and better until right around the 2010s. Phones did eventually stop getting way bigger, and camera bumps of course still grew, but now at this point we're basically maxing out how much phone you can carry in your pocket. Like there's basically no more room left in here. It's physics.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So the dramatic improvements were getting less dramatic. See these two photos? Pretty similar, right? Without zooming in and pixel peeping, they almost look like they could be taken with the same phone, just with a little contrast curve, saturation bump a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This one was taken with the iPhone 17. And this one was taken with the iPhone 11. Now, of course if you pixel peep, there are some more nuanced small differences, like if you're a photographer you probably already quickly noticed that the 17 has a larger sensor, that's why the background's a little bit more blurred, while the 11 has more in focus.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But generally for most people, they both look fine and basically any phone from the past five years can take a perfectly usable photo in broad daylight, perfect ideal conditions like this. So now what they're more focused on is getting better around the edges, like the more difficult shooting scenarios.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Low light, super fast-moving subjects, deep zoom, like these edge cases are where you can still see a bigger difference between a good and bad smartphone camera. So, you can start to see where this is going, right? Basically any phone these days can take a good enough quality photo in good lighting conditions, no matter the price. That doesn't separate them anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So the real separation between a good and a bad smartphone camera comes when the user throws the absolute worst possible conditions at it. So something like this photo right here. Right? This to an untrained eye, this might look like a pretty normal photo, which it should.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But if there's a photographer in the room watching this video over your shoulder, they've probably already pointed out that this scene is literally entirely backlit. And that smartphone, which happens to be a Pixel 10, is doing a ton of clever computational photography, multi-frame HDR, tone mapping, and...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Exposure adjustment, face detection, it's making sure somehow you can see my face and the black hoodie that I'm wearing and the blue sky behind me in one shot, which is actually insane.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: For some perspective, here's the same shot with a Nexus 4, which was before the days of smart HDR coming along. So that's how terrible the scene is supposed to look.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's like one of those self-correcting basketball hoops that Stuff Made Here made, the YouTuber, where no matter where you throw the ball, it goes in the hoop.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: That is what a modern smartphone camera has turned into. No matter what you point the camera at, you'll get a good picture. It refuses to spit out a bad one.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: To the point where you think there's no way your skills could be that bad because you never miss.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But this is where it's started to get weird. I think that in pursuit of this perfect photo-taking machine where you can't miss no matter how insane the situation is, it's actually started to make regular daylight photos slightly worse.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like you've probably noticed the over-processed look before. You've probably seen it here and there. Some brands are more guilty of it than others. It's a little bit hard to articulate what it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But I think in general, the more likely a brand or a phone camera is to throw every trick in the book to try to save a photo, the more likely they are to throw all of those same tricks at a regular photo that doesn't really need it. It kind of messes it up a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: See here, this is the progression of every Samsung Galaxy S ever taking the same photo, from S1 to S2, S3, S4, S5, all the way to today. And yes, they did skip S10 to S20 to line up with the year.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But you can specifically see around the Galaxy S8 when the window behind me is blown out, but then with the introduction of multi-frame HDR with the S9, suddenly you can see the blue sky and everything else is preserved.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Then keep going. S10, S20, S21, S22, all the way to today's phone, the S23, and I... I don't... I don't like this photo anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like, this is the exact over-processed look I'm talking about. Yes, you can see more than ever outside the window, the building and the sky outside, but there's also this kind of a haloing around the window and the plant next to the window now.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And yes, you can see more details than ever in my face, but my face is not actually that light.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So spin back to the Galaxy S23 and I don't think I'm the only one who prefers this older and maybe slightly worse photo because it just looks a little more natural.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There's a little less shadow detail and a little less HDR, but there's also less haloing and less unnatural glowing and flattening and it just overall looks better.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And I'm not the only one who thinks this. Look at the comments on the short that I published with all these photos in them. A lot of other people feel that way too.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So this is now the balancing act of tuning one of these cameras. I think a lot of these companies they already know, you know, they pull a lot of these tricks, the tone mapping, the multi-frame HDR, the face detection, the exposure adjustments.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And they know that that stuff in excess can look kind of weird on a regular photo. So it's all about knowing when to turn it up and when to turn it down. If you need all of that stuff to save a bad situation, then turn it up. But if you don't need it, turn it down.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Really the easiest way to appreciate this: every modern smartphone camera has this gap between what you see in the viewfinder as you're about to take the photo, and then once you take the photo, and then it processes, and then that new-looking photo.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Some take longer than others to process, but you can always see it snap into place. And the more difficult the shooting scenario, the more dramatic that snap. That is the post-processing that we're talking about here.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So look, if the iPhone 11 has more natural color than the iPhone 17, and the Galaxy S23 looks better in a lot of ways than the S26, then we know that some more tuning is definitely still needed.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There are some really good apps on these phones that will let you turn down the post-processing or even try to turn it completely off. I'll link some good ones below. Sometimes it works, sometimes it's a little bit more difficult, but you can try it if you want.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, I'll link all the shorts down below where you can watch all the generations of every smartphone through every single photo. Those are fun too. Thanks for watching. Catch you in the next one. Peace.

highlights: While smartphone hardware has largely hit its physical limit in size, the primary differentiator between modern cameras is now how they handle "edge cases" like low light, high motion, and extreme zoom.

Modern smartphone photography has become a "self-correcting" system where computational tricks like multi-frame HDR and tone mapping ensure a usable photo is produced regardless of the user's skill or lighting conditions.

The aggressive pursuit of saving difficult shots has led to an "over-processed" look in standard photos, where images can appear unnatural due to excessive sharpening, artificial haloing, and flattened shadows.

Many users actually prefer the look of older smartphone generations because they produce more natural colors and depth compared to the heavy-handed software processing found in the newest flagship models.

The visible "snap" or change that occurs between the phone's viewfinder and the final saved image illustrates the massive amount of post-processing software required to create a modern mobile photograph.
summary:
In this transcript, Marques Brownlee explores the evolution of smartphone cameras by comparing photos taken across every generation of the iPhone and Samsung Galaxy lines. He observes that while early generations saw massive year-over-year leaps in quality, hardware improvements have hit a plateau due to the physical size constraints of mobile devices. Consequently, in ideal lighting conditions, the differences between a five-year-old phone and the latest flagship are often negligible to the average user, as most modern devices have mastered basic photography.

The current frontier of smartphone innovation has shifted toward "computational photography"—the use of software to "save" photos in difficult scenarios like low light or heavy backlighting. Brownlee explains that features like multi-frame HDR and AI-driven exposure adjustments act like a "self-correcting basketball hoop," making it nearly impossible to take a technically "bad" photo. However, this reliance on software has led to a prominent theme of over-processing. By applying aggressive algorithms to every shot, newer phones often produce unnatural results characterized by "haloing," artificial skin brightening, and a loss of natural contrast.

Ultimately, the transcript highlights a paradoxical trend where newer devices can sometimes produce "worse" images than their predecessors because they lack a natural look. Brownlee notes that many users actually prefer photos from older models, such as the iPhone 11 or Galaxy S23, because they retain more realistic shadows and depth. He concludes that the future of smartphone photography lies in better "tuning"—the ability of a camera to recognize when to apply heavy processing and when to step back to preserve the natural aesthetic of a scene.
